title: Chicken Momos

overview.description: Steamed Himalayan dumplings made with homemade wheat dough and a juicy filling of minced chicken, onion, ginger, garlic and fresh coriander, served with a spicy tomato and chilli chutney.

ingredients: Small handful fresh coriander, finely chopped
2–4 dried red chillies, soaked in hot water
method: Place the flour and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a dough forms.
Knead for 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Cover the dough and rest for at least 30 minutes.
For the filling, place the chicken mince in a large bowl.
Add the onion, garlic, ginger, spring onions and fresh coriander.
Add the soy sauce, sesame oil, black pepper, cumin and chilli powder if using.
Add the neutral oil and mix thoroughly.
Season with salt if necessary.
Cover and refrigerate the filling while the dough rests.
Divide the dough into approximately 30–36 equal pieces.
Roll each piece into a ball.
Roll each ball into a thin round approximately 8–10cm in diameter.
Keep the prepared dough rounds covered so they do not dry out.
Place approximately 1 tablespoon of chicken filling in the centre of each dough round.
Moisten the edge with a little water.
Fold and pleat the dough around the filling to seal the momo.
Repeat with the remaining dough and filling.
Arrange the momos in a lightly oiled steamer, leaving space between them.
Steam for approximately 12–15 minutes, or until the dough is cooked and the chicken is completely cooked through.
For the chutney, soften the tomatoes by roasting, boiling or grilling them.
Blend the tomatoes, soaked chillies, garlic, lemon juice, salt and sesame oil until smooth or slightly textured.
Serve the hot momos immediately with the tomato-chilli chutney.

learning.did-you-know: Momos have strong Tibetan and Himalayan roots and became especially popular across northern India, Nepal and the Himalayan regions, where steamed dumplings are commonly served with intensely flavoured chilli chutneys.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a simple dumpling dough, roll wrappers by hand and prepare a juicy minced-meat filling suitable for steaming.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A freezer-friendly homemade dumpling that gives the family a Himalayan and northern Indian alternative to gyoza and mandu.
chapman-tips: The dough should be firm but supple rather than soft and sticky.
Resting the dough makes it much easier to roll.
Keep unused dough balls and rolled wrappers covered to prevent them drying out.
Don't roll the wrappers paper-thin; momos traditionally have a slightly thicker, chewier wrapper than gyoza.
The raw filling should be kept chilled while assembling.
A little oil in the filling helps keep the chicken moist.
Leave space between the momos in the steamer so they do not stick together.
The chutney can be made very hot or relatively mild depending on who is eating it.
Use a meat thermometer if desired; the chicken filling should reach a safe internal temperature.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Freeze uncooked momos in a single layer until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag or container. Steam directly from frozen, adding several minutes to the cooking time.
notes.leftovers: Cooked momos can be refrigerated and reheated by steaming. They can also be pan-fried after steaming.
